source_metadata.title_raw: Joan A. Borger - A-18-059 - May 11, 2018 - San Carlos

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Joan A. Borger 
Deputy City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
I 00 I Laurel Street, Suite A 
San Carlos, CA 94070 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A~18-059 
Dear Ms. Borger: 
May 11, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Foster City Mayor Sam Hindi 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note 
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under 
other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 
1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit the Mayor from taking part in governmental decisions relating to 
Costco's project to redevelop its retail warehouse facility within the City, including the installation 
of a gas station on site, given that the Mayor owns and operates a gas station within 0.41 miles of 
the Costco site? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his business interest in his gas station. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
Foster City has a resident population of approximately 30,000, with a larger daytime 
workforce population. The City comprises an area of approximately 4 square miles. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. AJl statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. AJI 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-059 
Page No. 2 
The Costco Corporation currently owns and operates a retail warehouse facility located at 
1001 Metro Center Boulevard within the City. The current Costco facility does not include a gas 
station. Costco has proposed the redevelopment of its existing facility at that site with a new, larger 
facility that will include a gas station with 32 fueling positions (the "Costco Project"). 
The Mayor owns and operates a gas station, the Hillsdale ARCO, located approximately 
0.41 miles (2,165 feet) away from the site of the Costco Project. The Mayor's gas station is one of 
four currently operating within the City, and the other three existing gas stations are closer to the 
Costco Project site than the Mayor's gas station. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision that would 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial 
interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The Act identifies an official's interests that may give rise to 
a disqualifying conflict of interest in Section 87103. Of those interests, the facts presented indicate 
that the Mayor has a business interest, including a source of income interest and a real property 
interest, in his gas station, and an interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family 
members, with respect to the decisions at issue. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 (b) provides that the effect of a decision on an official's interest that is not 
a named party in, or the subject of, that decision is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized 
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." The Mayor's gas station is not a 
named party in, or the subject of, the decisions relating to the Costco Project. Under this standard, it 
is a realistic possibility that the Mayor's gas station could experience increased competition from 
the potential Costco gas station. 
Looking to the applicable materiality standard for a decision's effect on a business entity not 
explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that the decision's effect on the 
official's business interest is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value 
of the privately-held business entity. That subdivision also provides examples of decisions that may 
have a material effect on an official's business interest, including a decision that would "[i]ncrease 
or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business is engaged." (Regulation 
18702.1 (b )(2).) 
Currently, there are only four gas stations in Foster City. The decisions relating to the 
Costco Project could result in the approval of a fifth gas station in the City. With 32 fueling 
positions, the potential Costco gas station is much larger than an average gas station. If the Costco 
Project is approved, it will increase the competition borne by gas stations within the City, including 
the Mayor's gas station. Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions relating to the Costco 
Project will contribute to a change in the value of the Mayor's gas station. Accordingly, the Act 


File No. A-18-059 
Page No. 3 
prohibits the Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on his business interest in the gas station.2 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
By: 
. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
2 We do not further analyze the Mayor's real property interest in his gas station or his interest in his personal 
finances because we have already determined that the decisions relating io the Costco Project would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material fmancial effect on his business interest in that gas station.

have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his business interest in his gas station.

0.41 miles (2,165 feet) away from the site of the Costco Project. The Mayor's gas station is one of 
four currently operating within the City, and the other three existing gas stations are closer to the

sections.analysis:
The Act prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision that would 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial 
interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The Act identifies an official's interests that may give rise to 
a disqualifying conflict of interest in Section 87103. Of those interests, the facts presented indicate 
that the Mayor has a business interest, including a source of income interest and a real property 
interest, in his gas station, and an interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family

Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 (b) provides that the effect of a decision on an official's interest that is not

Looking to the applicable materiality standard for a decision's effect on a business entity not 
explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that the decision's effect on the

Currently, there are only four gas stations in Foster City. The decisions relating to the

Project will contribute to a change in the value of the Mayor's gas station. Accordingly, the Act

Costco's project to redevelop its retail warehouse facility within the City, including the installation

the Costco site?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his business interest in his gas station. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR
